text: You can press some copper to the other side of the work piece, very useful on thin gauge, it will act as a heat sink and also help to stop oxygen getting into the weld from the other side

author: @Testacabeza
text: When I was a child my father made his own welder and made a lot of stuff around the house, stairs, gates, fixed doors, fixed the car, made us a merry-go-round, fixed our bikes, and a huge etc. He always said he wasn't real professional welder but you can get away with a lot of failures with practice, time, trial and error and the will to do stuff.

author: @superrodder2002
text: I would ad that if you ask around ,you will usually find someone who has welded for years that will spend a little time showing you the basics of striking an arc and manipulating the rod to control the weld puddle. A half hour with a good welder will save you hours of frustration trying to figure it out on your own

author: @pa_maj.MARTINI-van-MAN

replies.author: @pa_maj.MARTINI-van-MAN
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering With my welder I remember the weld sat higher ( raised ) meaning less penetration. My welder is a good one though and I've never tried with any other. Try on really thin metal with both methods, it's subtle but definitely should be noticeable if the welder is capable.

author: @William_Hada
text: Great tips Rob! I've done some MIG welding and find it very useful and fun to do. One helpful tip that I got from an expert is that good welds are all a matter of " Heat Control ".  Speed and power settings are adjusted so that you get good melting and fusing but not overheating and burn-through.

replies.author: @Kiyarose3999
replies.text: Yes just enough heat to maintain a steady pool of molten metal that the rod/wire is melted into.

author: @campbellpaul
text:
That's the way professionals actually weld.  They "tune" the welder as needed, they never go by the amperage on the machine dial or display, as it could be off, and often is.  You are spot-on about having control, too, as concentrating on proper form is essential to making consistently quality welds (stick-weld using both hands, unlike MIG welding where one hand often works fine).  
Most novice welders will sometimes need to do a structural-type weld once in a while, and beveling the plate that they are welding is essential to allow for deep penetration, and new welders should know how to perform a "root" pass on their welds as well.  Downhill welding can be tricky, as heat rises and moving uphill instead you will find to be better in many circumstances.  Great video, I always learn something from you. Oh, and never watch the arc while you are welding, as your eyes can't adjust to the dimness of where the weld-pool cools to the base metal.
